Document [6] (Title: Physiology, Spinal Cord -- Related Testing -- Lumbar Puncture) A lumbar puncture, or spinal tap, samples the CSF from the subarachnoid space. Contraindications to lumbar puncture include signs of cerebral herniation, focal neurological signs, uncorrected coagulopathies, or cardiorespiratory compromise. [21]

Document [11] (Title: Leptomeningeal Carcinomatosis -- Evaluation) If safe, then diagnostic evaluation should be furthered by a lumbar puncture (LP). In the case of LC, CSF studies usually show mild pleocytosis, hypoglycorrhachia (usually less than 60 mg/dL), and elevated protein (greater than 45 mg/dL). If the glucose levels are very low, then infectious etiologies must be ruled out. In 50% to 70% of cases, it may show elevated opening pressure (greater than 150 mm) as well. False-negative cytology results are common, and a study shows CSF cytology can have false-negative results of up to 36% when samples are refrigerated for 48 hours. These false-negative results can be minimized by securing a large volume (10 mL) of CSF for cytology, expediting sample processing without additional storage, and obtaining CSF from cisterns or the lumbar region or a site of known leptomeningeal involvement. In most cases, positive CSF studies and suggestive radiographic findings are enough to make a diagnosis, but a negative LP should be followed by at least one additional LP, especially if there is high clinical suspicion. The sensitivity of cytology is 50% to 60% after the first LP and approaches 85% to 90% with the second collection.

A dry tap is when the needle is within the spinal canal, but CSF does not flow. It occurs commonly with hospitalized old-age patients. It happens with epidural lipomatosis, arachnoiditis, and prior surgery, or severe spinal canal narrowing. Sometimes the CSF is too thick for the needle due to cells such as in infection or malignancy. The CSF is rechecked after asking patients to cough or performing Valsalva maneuvers. Document [19] (Title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 937) Hydrocephalus (استسقاء الدماغ) is recognized as a neurological disorder that involves the brain and nerves, and it is specifically identified within the brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway where excess cerebrospinal fluid accumulates. In the management of hydrocephalus, shunting procedures are commonly employed; the ventricular‑peritoneal shunt (التحويلة البطينية البريتونية) frequently co‑occurs with the condition and represents a specific type of shunt that diverts fluid from a cerebral ventricle to an extracranial site. This shunt is a child concept of the broader category “shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site,” which itself is nested under the procedural domain of insertion, replacement, or removal of extracranial ventricular shunts. Another variant, the ventricular shunt to the nasopharynx, is performed directly on the brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway and is considered closely related, and possibly synonymous, with the general insertion/replacement/removal procedure for extracranial ventricular shunts. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians diagnose hydrocephalus, anticipate its cognitive complications, and select appropriate shunting interventions for optimal patient care.

Document [23] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2226) Traumatisch hersenoedeem is a specific subtype of أوديما دماغية, meaning that traumatic brain edema is classified under the broader condition of cerebral edema. As a form of أوديما دماغية, Traumatisch hersenoedeem involves swelling of brain tissue that results from head injury, leading to increased intracranial pressure and potential neurological impairment. Document [26] (Title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 884) is identified as a possibly included method of exploration. Cranial exploration procedures include exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region, supratentorial craniectomy or craniotomy, and suboccipital craniectomy performed for exploration or decompression of cranial nerves. An orbit can be explored via a transcranial approach with lesion removal, which is likewise categorized as an exploration method. Spinal exploration is represented by laminectomy procedures that combine exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without additional facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy. These laminectomies are specified for one or two lumbar vertebral segments (e.g., for spinal stenosis) and are noted as possibly included methods. Additional laminectomy variants cover more extensive cervical or lumbar segments (more than two vertebral levels) and are also listed as possibly included methods of exploration. Other organ‑specific

Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2238) Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site is a neurosurgical procedure that falls under the broader category of insertion, replacement, or removal of extracranial ventricular shunt. Within this category, the specific technique known as التحويلة البطينية البريتونية is identified as a child procedure, representing a particular method of diverting cerebrospinal fluid from the cerebral ventricles to an extracranial location such as the peritoneal cavity. Recognizing that Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site is a subset of the general shunt management interventions and that التحويلة البطينية البريتونية is a specific variant of this procedure aids clinicians in selecting and performing the appropriate surgical approach for conditions like hydrocephalus.
